quotes.verbatim_quote: all dominant chords a Sth apart are related.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Tritone-substitution principle stated for dominant chord families.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Minor 6th type chords usually should not be used for a minor chord that is followed by a
dominant chord a 4th higher, because the m6 type chord will sound too much like the next

dominant chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specific avoidance rule for m6 in ii-V contexts.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The only altered minor chord that is widely used is the m75 type.

The dominant 11th chords (especially with no 3rd) are some of the prettiest chords when used
properly.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Highlights 11(no3) as a primary V-function substitution.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Augmented 11th type chords can be used in place of dominant chords that are followed by a
chord a 4th higher (just like other altered dominants).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Rule for +11 chord as altered-dominant substitute.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
the +11 does not assume a tonic
function; that is, you wouldn’t REPLACE a I7 at the beginning of a song with it.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Restricts altered dominants at tonic openings.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It is actually more
common to play a 9b5 than a +11.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Practical preference between two altered dominants.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Notice the use of common tones on top of some of these chords. This is one aid to smooth chord
resolutions.
Dominant suspended chords are usually used as partial replacements for dominant 7th chords
quotes.why_load_bearing: Rule for using sus dominants as duration-shared substitutions.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Sometimes suspended chords are left unresolved; in other words, they may directly replace the
dominant 7th chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Allows sus chord as standalone replacement.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The diminished 7th chord, usually referred to as the diminished chord, is a unique chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Frames diminished 7 as a special-case chord category.

quotes.verbatim_quote: any form of the diminished chord repeats itself every 3 frets.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Symmetry property; gives diminished chords 4 names.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Diminished chords are used as “passing” chords usually; that is, they go IN BETWEEN other
chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Functional role of diminished as connector.

quotes.verbatim_quote: When there is a great length of time given to the tonic major chord in a progression
quotes.why_load_bearing: Opens rule XII: how to vary an extended tonic.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Try leaving out the 3rd in major type chords for a different type of sound (expecially if you
replace the 3rd with a 2nd).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Voicing rule for sus-flavored major textures.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Major 7ths and major 9ths that have the root on top or 7th in the bass can be used as good
passing chords to a major type chord a 4th higher.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specific use of M7/M9 voicings as passing chords.
